title: Introduction to Information Security and Information Assurance
author: Dr. Zhijiang Chen
summary: Student-facing Week 1 deck aligned to the reading material, with detailed explanations, credible source-backed enrichment, applied examples, self-check prompts, and slide-specific technical line-art visuals.
external_sources: IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2025 — https://www.ibm.com/reports/data-breach
NIST Computer Security Resource Center Glossary — https://csrc.nist.gov/glossary

slides.title: Introduction to Information Security and Information Assurance
slides.main_idea: Security begins with a clear answer to three questions: what information matters, what could go wrong, and how can trust be preserved?
details: SCIA 120 Week 01 builds the vocabulary used throughout the course.
The module connects protection goals, attacker goals, risk, controls, and professional roles.
The focus is practical: students should be able to classify a real situation using security concepts.
slides.visual_kind: shield protecting connected data nodes
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Introduction to Information Security and Information Assurance. Visual must match this exact concept: shield protecting connected data nodes. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.why_it_matters: Every later topic—physical security, malware, cryptography, networks, secure coding, cloud security—depends on these Week 01 mental models.

slides.title: Week 01 Roadmap
slides.main_idea: The week moves from definitions to decision-making: define security goals, recognize threats, estimate risk, select controls, and verify outcomes.
details: Start with information security and information assurance.
Use CIA and DAD as paired models: defender goals vs. attacker outcomes.
Apply risk thinking before choosing controls.
Connect the security lifecycle to NIST CSF 2.0 and career pathways.
slides.visual_kind: four-stage roadmap with security icons
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Week 01 Roadmap. Visual must match this exact concept: four-stage roadmap with security icons. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A student-record system can be analyzed as assets, threats, CIA impact, controls, and assurance evidence.

slides.title: Learning Outcomes
slides.main_idea: By the end of this module, students should be able to explain and apply the basic language of information security.
details: Distinguish information security, cybersecurity, and information assurance.
Classify incidents by CIA and DAD properties.
Identify threat actors and likely motivations.
Use Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact to compare scenarios.
Match controls to risk and explain what evidence would show they work.
slides.visual_kind: checklist on secure clipboard
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Learning Outcomes. Visual must match this exact concept: checklist on secure clipboard. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.self_check: If you can read a short breach scenario and name the asset, CIA impact, likely actor, risk, control, and evidence, you are using the Week 01 toolkit.

slides.title: The Big Question: What Are We Defending?
slides.main_idea: Security work starts with assets, not tools. An asset is anything valuable enough to protect: data, systems, services, identities, operations, or trust.
details: Information can exist as database rows, files, emails, printed records, conversations, credentials, logs, and backups.
A system can fail security even without a hacker: accidental deletion, misconfiguration, power failure, or unavailable backups can still harm users.
Good security decisions name the protected asset before naming the technology.
slides.visual_kind: asset inventory map with data, identity, service, trust nodes
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: The Big Question: What Are We Defending?. Visual must match this exact concept: asset inventory map with data, identity, service, trust nodes. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: For an online gradebook, assets include grades, student identities, login credentials, audit logs, availability during registration, and trust in academic records.
slides.why_it_matters: If the asset is unclear, the control may protect the wrong thing.

slides.title: Information Security
slides.main_idea: Information security is the practice of protecting information and the systems that store, process, and transmit it from unauthorized access, use, disclosure, disruption, modification, or destruction.
details: It protects information while it is stored, being processed, and moving across networks.
The practical goals are privacy, accuracy, and availability for authorized users.
It includes people, processes, policies, and technology—not only firewalls or antivirus.
A useful security definition should lead to concrete controls such as encryption, access control, backups, monitoring, and training.
slides.visual_kind: data lifecycle with storage processing transmission
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Information Security. Visual must match this exact concept: data lifecycle with storage processing transmission. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
sources: NIST Computer Security Resource Center Glossary — https://csrc.nist.gov/glossary
slides.example: Encrypting a laptop protects stored information; MFA protects account access; backups protect availability after ransomware.
slides.why_it_matters: Information security turns abstract concern into decisions about what to protect and how.

slides.title: Cybersecurity vs. Information Security
slides.main_idea: Cybersecurity is usually the digital-systems subset of a broader information-security mission.
details: Cybersecurity focuses on networks, computers, software, cloud systems, devices, and digital attacks.
Information security also includes paper records, spoken information, physical files, human behavior, policy, and business processes.
The distinction matters because information can leak without a network attack: an unlocked office, misplaced printout, or overheard conversation can still be a security failure.
slides.visual_kind: nested circles: information security contains cybersecurity
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Cybersecurity vs. Information Security. Visual must match this exact concept: nested circles: information security contains cybersecurity. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A stolen database is cybersecurity and information security. A printed transcript left in a public hallway is information security even if no computer was hacked.
slides.why_it_matters: Professionals must protect information across its full lifecycle and all forms, not only inside computers.

slides.title: Information Assurance
slides.main_idea: Information assurance is about confidence: can users trust that information is available, accurate, authentic, confidential, and tied to accountable actions?
details: The reading expands assurance beyond protection to include authentication and non-repudiation.
Assurance depends on design, evidence, monitoring, auditing, continuity planning, and recovery capability.
A system may appear secure, but assurance asks whether there is proof that controls are operating correctly.
NIST and CISA guidance both emphasize governance, readiness, and repeatable practices—not one-time setup.
slides.visual_kind: assurance seal over verified logs and backup
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Information Assurance. Visual must match this exact concept: assurance seal over verified logs and backup. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A backup policy is security; test-restoring the backup and recording the result is assurance evidence.
slides.why_it_matters: Organizations need confidence before relying on information for medical, financial, government, or academic decisions.

slides.title: Security vs. Assurance
slides.main_idea: Security protects; assurance verifies, sustains, and proves that protection remains trustworthy over time.
details: Security question: what control reduces unauthorized disclosure, alteration, or denial?
Assurance question: how do we know that control is correctly implemented and still working?
Security often focuses on implementation; assurance adds measurement, governance, documentation, and accountability.
Both are required: a control without evidence is difficult to trust, and evidence without effective controls does not reduce risk.
slides.visual_kind: two-column protect versus prove diagram
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Security vs. Assurance. Visual must match this exact concept: two-column protect versus prove diagram. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: MFA reduces account takeover risk; logs showing MFA enrollment, failed login attempts, and periodic access reviews provide assurance.
slides.why_it_matters: Real organizations must defend systems and justify decisions to managers, auditors, regulators, and users.

slides.title: CIA Triad Overview
slides.main_idea: The CIA Triad is the classic model for the three core security properties: confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
details: Confidentiality asks: who is allowed to see this?
Integrity asks: is this accurate, complete, and unmodified?
Availability asks: can authorized users access it when needed?
Most incidents can be described as damage to one or more CIA properties.
slides.visual_kind: triangle with confidentiality integrity availability corners
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: CIA Triad Overview. Visual must match this exact concept: triangle with confidentiality integrity availability corners. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: Ransomware can affect availability by locking files, confidentiality by stealing data before encryption, and integrity by modifying systems.
slides.why_it_matters: CIA gives students a compact way to classify what went wrong and what controls are needed.

slides.title: Confidentiality
slides.main_idea: Confidentiality prevents unauthorized disclosure of information.
details: Sensitive information includes passwords, student records, health data, financial data, business plans, source code, and private communications.
Common threats include stolen credentials, misconfigured cloud storage, phishing, insider snooping, lost devices, and weak access control.
Controls include encryption, least privilege, MFA, access reviews, secure disposal, and data classification.
CISA Cyber Essentials emphasizes knowing who has access and using MFA, especially for privileged and remote users.
slides.visual_kind: locked document with authorized and blocked access paths
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Confidentiality. Visual must match this exact concept: locked document with authorized and blocked access paths. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A grade spreadsheet emailed to the wrong recipient is a confidentiality failure even if the file is not modified.
slides.why_it_matters: Confidentiality failures create legal, financial, reputational, and personal harm.

slides.title: Integrity
slides.main_idea: Integrity means information remains accurate, complete, and protected from unauthorized or accidental change.
details: Integrity failures can be malicious, such as altering grades or payment instructions, or accidental, such as database corruption.
Controls include hashes, checksums, digital signatures, version control, database constraints, change approvals, and audit logs.
Integrity is not only about preventing change; it is also about detecting and proving whether a change occurred.
Evidence matters: logs and hashes help reconstruct what changed and when.
slides.visual_kind: file hash fingerprint comparing original and changed file
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Integrity. Visual must match this exact concept: file hash fingerprint comparing original and changed file. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: If a student grade changes from 82 to 92 without authorization, the system has an integrity problem even if the record is still private and available.
slides.why_it_matters: Bad data can drive bad decisions: wrong medication, wrong payment, wrong grade, or wrong security response.

code.label: Tiny integrity demo
code.content:
sha256sum assignment.txt
# Change the file, then run again.
# A different hash means the contents changed.

slides.title: Availability
slides.main_idea: Availability means authorized users can access systems and information when they need them.
details: Availability can be harmed by DDoS attacks, ransomware, hardware failure, expired certificates, power loss, bad updates, or cloud outages.
Controls include backups, redundancy, failover, monitoring, patch management, disaster recovery plans, and capacity planning.
Availability is a security property because a perfectly private and accurate system still fails users if it is unreachable.
CISA Cyber Essentials emphasizes protecting critical assets and preparing for crisis response.
slides.visual_kind: service uptime monitor with redundant servers
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Availability. Visual must match this exact concept: service uptime monitor with redundant servers. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A hospital scheduling system that is offline during emergency operations creates real-world harm even if no data is stolen.
slides.why_it_matters: For many organizations, downtime means lost revenue, safety risk, missed deadlines, and loss of public trust.

code.label: Simple availability check
code.content:
ping -c 3 example.edu
# Replies suggest network reachability; failures need diagnosis.

slides.title: DAD Triad: The Attacker’s Mirror
slides.main_idea: The DAD Triad describes attacker outcomes: disclosure, alteration, and denial—the opposites of confidentiality, integrity, and availability.
details: Disclosure violates confidentiality by exposing information to unauthorized parties.
Alteration violates integrity by changing information or system state without authorization.
Denial violates availability by preventing legitimate use.
DAD helps students translate incidents into attacker impact.
slides.visual_kind: mirror diagram mapping CIA to DAD
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: DAD Triad: The Attacker’s Mirror. Visual must match this exact concept: mirror diagram mapping CIA to DAD. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A ransomware incident may involve disclosure through data theft, alteration through encrypted files, and denial through system outage.
slides.why_it_matters: Defenders preserve CIA; attackers try to create DAD. The paired model makes incident analysis easier.

slides.title: Why Security Matters
slides.main_idea: Security failures matter because they create real costs for people, organizations, and society.
details: Financial impact includes incident response, legal fees, fines, customer notification, downtime, and lost trust.
Personal impact includes identity theft, exposure of medical records, privacy violations, and fraud.
National-security impact appears when critical infrastructure, elections, defense, or public services are targeted.
IBM’s 2025 breach report emphasizes that governance gaps—especially around fast AI adoption—can increase breach risk and cost.
slides.visual_kind: impact wheel: financial personal legal national trust
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Why Security Matters. Visual must match this exact concept: impact wheel: financial personal legal national trust. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
sources: IBM Cost of a Data Breach Report 2025 — https://www.ibm.com/reports/data-breach
slides.example: A breach of a campus system could expose identities, interrupt services, require notification, trigger investigation, and damage trust in institutional systems.
slides.why_it_matters: Security is not just a technical preference; it is risk management for real harm.

slides.title: A Brief History of Computer Security
slides.main_idea: Computer security evolved as computing moved from shared mainframes to personal computers, the internet, organized cybercrime, cloud services, and AI-enabled systems.
details: 1960s–1970s: timesharing raised early questions about user separation and unauthorized access.
1980s: PC malware and the Morris Worm showed that self-replicating code could disrupt connected systems.
1990s: the web and email expanded attack surfaces and fraud opportunities.
2000s–present: organized cybercrime, ransomware, nation-state operations, cloud, mobile, IoT, and supply-chain attacks changed the scale of risk.
slides.visual_kind: timeline from mainframe to cloud and AI
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: A Brief History of Computer Security. Visual must match this exact concept: timeline from mainframe to cloud and AI. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: The Morris Worm is often used as an early lesson in how software flaws, trust assumptions, and network connectivity can turn a mistake into widespread disruption.
slides.why_it_matters: History shows that security problems follow technology adoption: every new platform creates new attack surfaces.

slides.title: Threat Actors: Capability, Intent, Opportunity
slides.main_idea: A threat actor is any person, group, or entity with the potential to harm information systems.
details: Capability means the actor has tools, skills, access, or resources.
Intent means the actor has a reason to act: money, ideology, coercion, ego, espionage, revenge, or negligence.
Opportunity means a weakness is reachable: exposed service, weak password, unlocked device, misconfiguration, or trusted access.
Defenses should match the actor: a control that stops casual misuse may not stop a well-funded APT.
slides.visual_kind: threat actor radar chart capability intent opportunity
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Threat Actors: Capability, Intent, Opportunity. Visual must match this exact concept: threat actor radar chart capability intent opportunity. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A weak password creates opportunity for many actors, but a nation-state and a script kiddie differ greatly in patience, tools, and target selection.
slides.why_it_matters: Threat modeling becomes clearer when students ask who might attack, why, and with what capability.

slides.title: Common Threat Actor Profiles
slides.main_idea: Different threat actors behave differently because their skills, goals, and resources differ.
details: Script kiddies use existing tools with limited understanding and often seek curiosity, disruption, or status.
Hacktivists use cyber actions to advance political, social, or ideological goals.
Cybercriminals pursue profit through ransomware, credential theft, fraud, extortion, and stolen-data markets.
These actors often reuse known vulnerabilities, leaked credentials, and social engineering because those methods scale.
slides.visual_kind: three actor cards with tools motives targets
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Common Threat Actor Profiles. Visual must match this exact concept: three actor cards with tools motives targets. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A phishing kit sold online may allow low-skill attackers to steal credentials without understanding the underlying web or authentication technology.
slides.why_it_matters: Knowing the profile helps predict likely tactics and prioritize controls.

slides.title: Insiders and Nation-State/APT Actors
slides.main_idea: Some of the hardest threats come from trusted access or patient, well-funded adversaries.
details: Insider threats come from employees, contractors, vendors, or partners with legitimate access; harm may be malicious or negligent.
Nation-state actors may pursue espionage, strategic advantage, sabotage, or influence operations.
Advanced Persistent Threats are long-term, targeted campaigns that prioritize stealth and persistence.
Controls must include least privilege, monitoring, segmentation, logging, and incident response readiness.
slides.visual_kind: insider badge and external APT path converging on assets
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Insiders and Nation-State/APT Actors. Visual must match this exact concept: insider badge and external APT path converging on assets. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: An employee copying customer data to a personal drive is an insider risk; a long-term stealth campaign against a supplier may indicate APT-style behavior.
slides.why_it_matters: Perimeter defenses are not enough when the actor may already have access or time to adapt.

slides.title: Attack Motivations: MICE
slides.main_idea: MICE summarizes common motivations: Money, Ideology, Coercion, and Ego.
details: Money drives ransomware, fraud, credential theft, extortion, and data resale.
Ideology drives hacktivism, defacement, leaks, and disruption for a cause.
Coercion appears when someone acts under pressure, blackmail, or threat.
Ego drives bragging, revenge, status seeking, or the thrill of access.
Motivation helps estimate what the actor may target and how persistent they may be.
slides.visual_kind: MICE motivation matrix with examples
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Attack Motivations: MICE. Visual must match this exact concept: MICE motivation matrix with examples. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A ransomware group usually wants money; a hacktivist group may want attention; an insider may be motivated by revenge, coercion, or financial gain.
slides.why_it_matters: Motivation changes likely targets, timing, tactics, and negotiation behavior.

slides.title: The Security Mindset
slides.main_idea: The security mindset means looking at systems through failure, abuse, and trust assumptions—not only through intended use.
details: Adversarial thinking asks: how could this be misused?
Skepticism treats inputs, identities, and assumptions as things to verify.
Failure-mode thinking asks what happens when a control breaks or a user makes a mistake.
Defense in depth assumes no single control is perfect.
Proportionality balances risk reduction against cost, complexity, and usability.
slides.visual_kind: system diagram with intended path and abuse path
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: The Security Mindset. Visual must match this exact concept: system diagram with intended path and abuse path. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A login form should be designed not only for correct passwords, but also for guessing attacks, stolen credentials, error-message leaks, lockout abuse, and logging.
slides.why_it_matters: Security professionals find problems by asking different questions than ordinary users or developers.

slides.title: Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact
slides.main_idea: Risk is the potential for loss when a threat can exploit a vulnerability and cause impact.
details: Threat: what harmful event or actor could occur?
Vulnerability: what weakness could be exploited?
Impact: how severe is the harm if exploitation succeeds?
The formula is conceptual, not exact arithmetic, but it forces structured thinking.
Risk can be reduced by lowering likelihood, removing vulnerability, or reducing impact.
slides.visual_kind: risk equation cards multiplying threat vulnerability impact
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Risk = Threat × Vulnerability × Impact. Visual must match this exact concept: risk equation cards multiplying threat vulnerability impact. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: An internet-facing unpatched server with sensitive customer data has high risk because threat likelihood, exploitable vulnerability, and impact are all significant.
slides.why_it_matters: Risk thinking prevents random control selection. It connects security spending to actual harm reduction.

code.label: Simple risk scoring idea
code.content:
risk_score = threat_likelihood × vulnerability_exposure × impact
Use it to compare scenarios, not as perfect math.

slides.title: Risk Treatment Options
slides.main_idea: After identifying risk, organizations choose how to handle it: avoid, mitigate, transfer, or accept.
details: Avoid: stop the risky activity entirely.
Mitigate: reduce likelihood or impact with controls.
Transfer: shift some financial impact to another party, such as insurance or a service provider contract.
Accept: document the risk and choose not to act when it is low or treatment cost is disproportionate.
Good risk decisions should be explicit, not accidental.
slides.visual_kind: four-way risk treatment decision board
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Risk Treatment Options. Visual must match this exact concept: four-way risk treatment decision board. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: Turning off an unused exposed service avoids risk; patching it mitigates risk; cyber insurance transfers some financial risk; documenting a low-risk issue accepts it.
slides.why_it_matters: Security teams rarely eliminate all risk. They help organizations choose defensible treatment strategies.

slides.title: Control Functions: Preventive, Detective, Corrective
slides.main_idea: Controls reduce risk by stopping incidents, finding incidents, or helping recover from incidents.
details: Preventive controls stop or block unwanted events: MFA, encryption, firewall rules, secure configuration.
Detective controls reveal events or suspicious activity: logs, IDS alerts, monitoring, file integrity checks.
Corrective controls limit damage and restore operations: backups, patches, account resets, incident response procedures.
Strong programs combine all three because prevention eventually fails.
slides.visual_kind: three-layer control pipeline prevent detect correct
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Control Functions: Preventive, Detective, Corrective. Visual must match this exact concept: three-layer control pipeline prevent detect correct. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: For phishing: MFA is preventive, suspicious-login alerts are detective, password reset and session revocation are corrective.
slides.why_it_matters: Classifying controls helps students understand what a defense actually does.

slides.title: Control Layers: Administrative, Technical, Physical
slides.main_idea: Controls also differ by implementation layer: policy and process, technology, or physical protection.
details: Administrative controls include policies, training, hiring procedures, acceptable-use rules, and incident response plans.
Technical controls include authentication, encryption, access control, endpoint protection, logging, and network segmentation.
Physical controls include locks, cameras, guards, badges, secure rooms, and cable locks.
Effective defense in depth uses multiple layers because one layer alone is fragile.
slides.visual_kind: stacked layers administrative technical physical
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Control Layers: Administrative, Technical, Physical. Visual must match this exact concept: stacked layers administrative technical physical. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: Protecting a server room may require badge policy, access-control logs, locked doors, cameras, and system authentication—not just one control.
slides.why_it_matters: A technically strong system can still fail if physical access or organizational process is weak.

slides.title: Security Lifecycle and NIST CSF 2.0
slides.main_idea: Security is a continuous lifecycle, not a one-time project. NIST CSF 2.0 organizes cybersecurity outcomes around Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, and Recover.
details: Govern sets strategy, accountability, policy, and risk management direction.
Identify understands assets, dependencies, threats, vulnerabilities, and risk.
Protect implements safeguards to reduce likelihood or impact.
Detect finds possible cybersecurity events.
Respond contains and manages incidents; Recover restores operations and improves resilience.
The cycle repeats because threats, systems, and business needs change.
slides.visual_kind: six-step circular lifecycle with NIST CSF labels
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Security Lifecycle and NIST CSF 2.0. Visual must match this exact concept: six-step circular lifecycle with NIST CSF labels. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: A school system identifies student-record assets, protects them with access control, detects suspicious logins, responds to compromised accounts, recovers affected services, and updates policy under governance.
slides.why_it_matters: The framework gives students a professional vocabulary used by organizations beyond this course.

slides.title: Student Scenario: Classify the Incident
slides.main_idea: Apply the Week 01 toolkit to a realistic incident instead of memorizing terms in isolation.
details: Scenario: a staff member receives a phishing email, enters credentials into a fake page, and an attacker downloads a spreadsheet of student records.
Asset: student records and account credentials.
CIA impact: confidentiality is violated; integrity and availability may also be at risk if the attacker changes or deletes data.
Threat actor: likely cybercriminal unless evidence suggests another motive.
Controls: MFA, phishing-resistant training, login anomaly detection, least privilege, access review, and incident response.
Evidence: sign-in logs, MFA logs, email headers, file access logs, affected-account timeline.
slides.visual_kind: incident analysis worksheet with asset CIA actor controls evidence
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Student Scenario: Classify the Incident. Visual must match this exact concept: incident analysis worksheet with asset CIA actor controls evidence. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.why_it_matters: Scenario practice builds the habit of moving from vocabulary to analysis.
slides.self_check: Can you identify DAD impact, risk treatment, and at least one preventive, detective, and corrective control for this scenario?

slides.title: Cybersecurity Career Paths
slides.main_idea: Week 01 concepts appear in many security roles, from technical operations to governance and leadership.
details: Security Analyst: monitors alerts, investigates suspicious activity, and supports response.
Penetration Tester: performs authorized testing to find weaknesses before attackers do.
Security Engineer: builds and maintains secure systems and infrastructure.
Incident Responder / Forensic Analyst: investigates incidents, preserves evidence, and identifies root cause.
Security Architect: designs security posture across systems.
CISO and GRC roles connect security to risk, policy, compliance, and executive decisions.
ISC2’s 2025 study emphasizes demand for both technical and nontechnical skills, including incident response, security engineering, risk, cloud, AI, and GRC.
slides.visual_kind: career pathway map from foundations to roles
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Cybersecurity Career Paths. Visual must match this exact concept: career pathway map from foundations to roles. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.example: The same CIA/risk/control vocabulary is used in SOC tickets, penetration test reports, architecture reviews, audits, and executive risk briefings.
slides.why_it_matters: A strong foundation helps students move toward certifications and roles such as Security+, CEH, CISSP, OSCP, analyst, engineer, responder, or GRC analyst.

slides.title: Week 01 Takeaway
slides.main_idea: Security is a process for preserving trust in information under uncertainty, attack, failure, and change.
details: Start every analysis with assets and context.
Use CIA to name defender goals and DAD to name attacker outcomes.
Estimate risk through threat, vulnerability, and impact.
Choose controls by function and layer, then look for evidence that they work.
Use the lifecycle mindset: Govern, Identify, Protect, Detect, Respond, Recover.
Keep building the security mindset: ask what can fail, who could abuse it, and what proof would increase confidence.
slides.visual_kind: summary constellation connecting CIA DAD risk controls lifecycle careers
slides.visual_prompt: Technical line-art design for SCIA 120 Week 01 slide: Week 01 Takeaway. Visual must match this exact concept: summary constellation connecting CIA DAD risk controls lifecycle careers. Use abstract topic-specific cybersecurity symbols; avoid photorealism.
slides.why_it_matters: These concepts are the foundation for the rest of SCIA 120.
slides.self_check: Explain one recent security incident using: asset, CIA impact, DAD impact, threat actor, motivation, vulnerability, impact, controls, and assurance evidence.
